questions.question: Explain the difference between sequential access and random access file handling in C. Give one advantage of each method.
keyPoints: Sequential access reads/writes records in order, requiring traversal of all preceding data
Random access uses fseek() to directly position to any record
Sequential access advantage: simpler implementation and better for batch processing
Random access advantage: O(1) access time for any record

questions.question: Write a C program to find the size of a file using ftell() and fseek() functions.
keyPoints: Use fseek() to move to end of file: fseek(fp, 0, SEEK_END)
Use ftell() to get current position which equals file size
Reset pointer using rewind() or fseek() for further operations
Close file after use

questions.question: Describe the purpose and syntax of fseek() function with all its parameters.
keyPoints: Purpose: moves file pointer to specified location
Syntax: int fseek(FILE *stream, long offset, int origin)
offset: number of bytes from origin
origin: SEEK_SET (0), SEEK_CUR (1), or SEEK_END (2)
Returns 0 on success, non-zero on failure

questions.question: Why must files be opened in binary mode for random access operations? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Text mode translates newline characters (\n to \r\n on Windows)
This translation causes inconsistency in byte positions
Binary mode reads/writes exact bytes without translation
Example: 10 newlines in text mode may occupy 20 bytes on Windows but only 10 in binary mode

questions.question: Explain the three file positioning functions: fseek(), ftell(), and rewind() with their return types and purposes.
keyPoints: fseek(int): moves pointer to specific location, returns 0 on success
ftell(long): returns current pointer position in bytes from start
rewind(void): moves pointer to beginning, no return value
All are used for random access file manipulation

questions.question: A file contains 50 records of type struct Student { int roll; char name[30]; float marks; }. Write the C code to read the 25th record directly.
keyPoints: Define Student structure
Open file in binary read mode (rb)
Calculate offset: (25-1) * sizeof(Student) = 24 * sizeof(Student)
Use fseek() to position to the record
Use fread() to read the record
Close file

questions.question: Write a complete C program that demonstrates random access file handling by creating a file with employee records (containing emp_id, name, salary), allowing the user to search a record by emp_id and display it, and modify the salary of a specific employee.
keyPoints: Define EMPLOYEE structure with appropriate fields
Create file and write initial records using fwrite()
Implement search: iterate or calculate position, use fseek() to reach record
Display record details after reading with fread()
Implement modify: fseek() to record position, overwrite with fwrite()
Handle file errors and validate operations
Use rewind() or fseek() to reset pointer as needed

questions.question: Explain the concept of random access file handling in C with reference to fseek(), ftell(), and rewind() functions. Write a program to display all records of a file in reverse order without loading them into memory.
keyPoints: fseek(): positions pointer using offset and origin (SEEK_SET/CUR/END)
ftell(): returns current position, use to find file size
rewind(): resets pointer to beginning
To display in reverse: find file size, calculate number of records
Seek to last record, read and display, move backward using fseek()
Each record accessed in O(1) time, total O(n) for n records
No array needed, reads one record at a time

questions.question: Create a banking application in C that uses random access file handling to manage customer accounts. The application should support creating new accounts, displaying account details by account number, depositing money, and withdrawing money.
keyPoints: Define ACCOUNT structure with acc_no, name, balance
Create account: append at end using fseek(0, SEEK_END) and fwrite()
Display: calculate position using (acc_no-1)*sizeof(ACCOUNT), fseek(), fread()
Deposit: read record, add amount, fseek() back, fwrite()
Withdraw: similar to deposit with balance validation
Use fflush() after write operations to ensure data is written to disk
Menu-driven interface with error handling
